questions.q: The trapezoidal rule approximates ∫[a,b] f(x)dx by treating the region under f as a trapezoid. What is its local truncation error order?
options: O(h³) where h is the step size
O(h) where h is the step size
O(h²) where h is the step size
O(h⁴) where h is the step size
questions.explanation: The trapezoidal rule has local error O(h³) per interval, but globally (compositing over n intervals) it becomes O((b-a)h²) = O(1/n²). Simpson's rule is O(h⁵) locally and O(1/n⁴) globally.

questions.q: Simpson's rule uses a parabola through three points. For n subintervals with even n, how many function evaluations are needed?
options: n + 1 evaluations
2n function evaluations
n/2 + 1 evaluations
3n evaluations
questions.explanation: Simpson's rule composites n/2 parabolic pieces, each using 3 points. The points are shared between adjacent intervals, so evaluations at interior points (x₁, x₂, ..., x_{n-1}) are counted once. Total: 1 + n = n + 1 points.

questions.q: Gaussian quadrature optimally places quadrature nodes and weights. For n points, what is the highest polynomial degree it integrates exactly?
options: Degree n
Degree 2n + 1
Degree 2n - 1
Degree n²
questions.explanation: Gaussian quadrature with n nodes integrates polynomials of degree up to 2n - 1 exactly. The nodes are chosen as roots of orthogonal polynomials (e.g., Legendre polynomials), and weights are computed to maximize precision.

questions.q: Which integration method is most affected by oscillatory or rapidly varying integrands?
options: Gaussian quadrature, which assumes smooth functions and few nodes
Trapezoidal rule with equispaced nodes
Simpson's rule with adaptive refinement
Clenshaw-Curtis quadrature with Chebyshev nodes
questions.explanation: Trapezoidal rule with fixed equispaced nodes struggles with oscillatory integrands because it may miss peaks. Gaussian quadrature and Clenshaw-Curtis can be effective even for oscillatory functions. Adaptive Simpson's rule refines in high-variation regions.

questions.q: Adaptive quadrature divides an interval and refines where the error is largest. What is a typical stopping criterion?
options: Subdivide until the difference between coarse and fine estimates is below tolerance ε
Always subdivide to a maximum depth of 20
Stop when the function has been evaluated at 10,000 points
Subdivide until all subintervals have length < 0.001
questions.explanation: Adaptive methods estimate error via difference between estimates (e.g., Simpson vs. nested Simpson or trapezoidal vs. Simpson). If error < ε, the subinterval is accepted; otherwise it is subdivided. This efficiently allocates function evaluations.

questions.q: For a function with a singularity at x = a inside [a, b], how should integration be handled?
options: Apply a change of variables or split the interval and use weighted quadrature designed for singularities
Simply apply Simpson's rule across the singularity
Exclude a small neighborhood around x = a
Use bisection to find the exact singularity location
questions.explanation: Singularities require special treatment: variable substitution can remove the singularity, or specialized quadrature rules (e.g., Gauss-Laguerre) can handle them. Naive Simpson's rule fails because smoothness assumptions are violated.

questions.q: The global error of the composite trapezoidal rule for n subintervals is O(1/n²). To reduce error by a factor of 100, approximately how many more subintervals are needed?
options: 10 times more subintervals
100 times more subintervals
1,000 times more subintervals
5 times more subintervals
questions.explanation: If E_n ≈ C/n², then E_{10n} ≈ C/(100n²) = E_n/100. Multiplying n by 10 reduces error by 100. This illustrates how quadratic convergence requires many more evaluations for high precision.

questions.q: What is the main advantage of Gaussian quadrature over Newton-Cotes formulas (trapezoidal, Simpson's)?
options: Optimal node placement and weights for a given number of function evaluations; higher polynomial precision
Easier to implement without computing orthogonal polynomials
Superior handling of discontinuities in the integrand
No need to specify the integration interval
questions.explanation: Gaussian quadrature nodes are optimally placed (as roots of orthogonal polynomials), not equispaced. With n nodes, Gaussian quadrature integrates degree 2n - 1 polynomials exactly, vs. degree n - 1 for Newton-Cotes. This makes it far more accurate for smooth functions.

questions.q: Multiple integration (e.g., 2D) using tensor products of 1D quadrature rules requires how many function evaluations for n nodes per dimension in 2D?
options: n² evaluations
2n evaluations
n evaluations
n³ evaluations
questions.explanation: A 2D tensor product quadrature rule uses a Cartesian grid of nodes: all combinations of n x-nodes and n y-nodes, totaling n × n = n² points. In d dimensions, this grows as n^d, causing the curse of dimensionality.

questions.q: Why does the trapezoidal rule yield exact results for linear functions f(x) = mx + c?
options: The parabola through any three points on a line is the line itself; the area under the trapezoid equals the exact integral
Rounding errors cancel out for linear functions
The derivative of a linear function is constant, reducing error
Linear functions have no second derivative, so error bounds give E = 0
questions.explanation: Trapezoidal rule connects the function values with straight lines (degree ≤ 1 interpolation). For a linear function, this interpolant is exact, so the numerical integral equals the analytical result. This is why the method has at least degree-1 precision.
